_note: HOW TO USE: Place working papers in 'working_papers' and published papers in 'published'. See README.md for the promotion guide.

working_papers.title: Algorithmic Nudges and Political Attitude Formation: Evidence from a Large-Scale Field Experiment
working_papers.abstract: We study how recommendation algorithm variations on a major social media platform influence downstream political attitude formation using a pre-registered randomized experiment with 18,000 participants. Our findings suggest that even minor ranking changes produce measurable shifts in issue salience that persist for up to six weeks post-exposure.
working_papers.status: Under Review at Nature Human Behaviour

working_papers.title: When Corrections Backfire: Platform Labeling Interventions and Engagement in Low-Trust Populations
working_papers.abstract: Information labels on social media are increasingly deployed to combat misinformation, but their effectiveness may vary by audience trust in the labeling authority. Drawing on a series of survey experiments, we find that corrections issued by sources perceived as politically partisan can increase sharing of labeled content among ideologically aligned users.

published.title: Search as a Signal: How Query Volume Predicts Offline Behavioral Change During Public Health Crises
published.abstract: We leverage a novel dataset of anonymized search queries paired with administrative health records to show that aggregate search behavior predicted vaccination uptake 3–5 weeks earlier than traditional surveillance signals.

published.title: The Asymmetric Visibility of Outrage: Emotional Content Moderation and Cross-Platform Amplification
published.journal: Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction (CSCW)
published.abstract: Using a multi-platform content tracking pipeline, we demonstrate that emotionally charged content removed from one platform is re-shared cross-platform at higher rates than non-removed content, with amplification patterns dependent on the political alignment of originating communities.
